text: The surgeon carefully frees omental adhesions from the cecum to improve exposure of the omentum over the liver and transverse colon.
The surgeon begins inferior mobilization of the colon, carefully checking the ureter due to its proximity to the dissection area.

The surgeon performs inferior lateral mobilization to enhance visibility of the medial aspect of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and clear medial lymph node tissue, using a technique aimed at improving comfort with the complete mesocolic excision (CME) approach.

The surgeon performs inferior mobilization, checking the ureter for proximity during dissection. The tattoo marking the cancer is visible in the mid-ascending colon. The dissection progresses along the root of the ileal mesentery towards the fourth portion of the duodenum and the ligament of Treitz, with the duodenum becoming visible at this level.

The surgeon navigates over the duodenum, reaching the cephalic extent of the dissection with the current port setup and camera position. The positioning and changes between the surgical arms are visible, avoiding contact with the iliocolic pedicle.

The surgeon navigates over the right kidney towards the inferior edge of the liver, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and right side up. Despite the challenging positioning, the surgeon maintains good visualization to continue the dissection.

The surgeon switches to a medial to lateral dissection, elevating the iliocolic pedicle. The dissection proceeds underneath, joining the medially and laterally dissected planes. The surgeon approaches the junction of the ileocolic artery and vein with the superior mesenteric artery and vein.

The surgeon performs a CME dissection, focusing on lymphatic tissue as they progress higher. The ileocolic vein is visible, with the superior mesenteric vein positioned posterior and lateral to the superior mesenteric artery. The surgeon approaches the superior mesenteric artery to join the iliocolic artery, opting not to extensively clear fat from the anterior surface of the vessel.

The surgeon encounters bleeding after inadvertently contacting a branch of the loop of Henle. The ileocolic artery obstructs the view, but the surgeon manages to reposition it for better exposure. The surgical field is adjusted to gain control over the bleeding, preventing retraction into the pancreas. The surgeon uses electrocautery on the head of the pancreas to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a clean sponge to manage bleeding while continuing the dissection near the origin of the vessels using the CME technique. The surgical field is carefully manipulated to avoid errors during this critical step.

The surgeon approaches the superior mesenteric artery, which is positioned anterior to the vein, and navigates through the fatty tissue to identify the right branch of the middle colic artery. The superior mesenteric vein is visible as the surgeon dissects tissue from its medial aspect.

The surgeon takes the right branch of the middle colic artery, leaving some fatty tissue intact due to the junction cecum of ascending colon tumor. The surface of the superior mesenteric artery is visible, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the T-colon mesentery up to the mid-transverse colon at the level of the false front ligament.

The surgeon uses two right-hand instruments to lower the camera in the field, enhancing exposure. The view shows a marginal branch approaching the colon wall, with the surgical field extending from the left upper quadrant to the suprapubic area, where a 12mm port is used for stapling.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the planned transection level of the colon, keeping it with the specimen. The camera positioned in the left lower quadrant provides a challenging view of the hepatic flexure. The patient is repositioned using table motion to improve visibility. The surgeon proceeds to dissect through the gastrocolic and gastrohepatic omentum, carefully separating tissue from the stomach and pylorus while frequently checking the proximity to these structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection at the hepatic flexure, detaching the colon from its attachments near the superior aspect of the right kidney. With further manipulation, the view improves, revealing the completed lateral mobilization of the colon.

The surgeon uses a stapler inserted through the suprapubic twelve millimeter port to staple the small bowel mesentery, preparing for specimen extraction. The approach involves taking the rest of the small bowel mesentery from the ligament of Treitz down to the root of the ileal mesentery in the right lower quadrant.

The surgeon places the specimen above the liver and examines the dissection area, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and the accompanying vein, identifiable by its blue coloration. The surgeon prepares for an isoparastaltic anastomosis, opting to sew the colon anastomosis instead of using a stapler.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis on the right colon, placing a stay suture at the far end to align the anastomosis and elevate the bowel for better exposure. The camera and working arms are optimally positioned, allowing for effective suturing. The surgeon creates an outer back row using a two-layer technique with a 3-0 V-Loc suture, preparing to make an enterotomy.

The surgeon creates a colotomy and begins the inner back row of sutures, starting from the center and sewing away from themselves. They transition the suture to form the inner anterior row, inverting the mucosal edges. A second stitch is placed in the center, sewing towards the near side.

The surgeon takes larger canal bites on the colotomy side, which is visibly larger than the enterotomy side, while suturing the anastomosis. The suturing technique is adjusted for tissue thickness to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon completes a full circle two-layer anastomosis by sewing the original posterior outer suture over the top, visually forming a continuous circular pattern.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the suprapubic port site and brings the omentum down to cover the mesenteric defects without closing them.
